findings.justification: actionIndex performs only a nonce check (validateToken) with no capability check; the wp_ajax_smart-slider3 hook dispatcher (display_admin_ajax, AdminHelper.php line 155) skips the checkForCap() guard that all other handlers use, so any logged-in WordPress user (e.g. subscriber) can enumerate server-side directories and filenames under the images folder.
The AJAX response includes the absolute server filesystem path as 'fullPath', leaking the server's directory layout to any caller who can reach the endpoint.

When the realpath()-resolved path falls outside $root, both handlers re-assign $path to $originalFullPath (the unresolved, attacker-controlled string) if any ancestor is a symlink, without re-verifying that the symlink target remains within $root, allowing a symlink placed inside the images folder to redirect directory listing or file uploads to arbitrary server paths.

SVG files are accepted for upload (mime image/svg+xml) and written to disk via BulletProof without any XML/SVG sanitization to strip embedded <script> or event-handler attributes, enabling stored XSS if the SVG is later served with an inline content type or embedded in a page.

After the N2_IMAGE_UPLOAD_DISABLE check and current_user_can check both call $this->response->error(), execution falls through to $this->validateToken() and the upload logic if ResponseAjax::error() does not terminate the PHP process (though PageFlow::exitApplication is called, any mis-implementation or output buffering issue could allow continuation).
